hero.ariaLabel: A scenic nature landscape at sunset showing an ecosystem environment

heroCopy.lede: On screen this looks like a modern lesson page — but when you print it, it becomes a worksheet. Read, observe, then write your explanations using evidence from the images and quick facts.
heroCopy.whatStudentsLearn: Students learn what an ecosystem is, how living and nonliving parts work together, and why living things depend on each other. They explore interdependence (like pollination), what happens when ecosystems change, and how to design their own ecosystem using resources, relationships, and survival needs.
learningTargets: I can explain what an ecosystem is (living and nonliving parts together).
I can identify biotic (living) and abiotic (nonliving) factors.
I can describe how living things depend on each other for survival.
I can explain how changes can affect an ecosystem.
studentDirections: Read each section and study the picture.
Use evidence from the quick facts to answer.
On paper: underline key vocabulary words.

keyVocabulary.def: a community of living things and the nonliving parts of their environment working together.
living parts of an ecosystem (plants, animals, fungi, bacteria).
nonliving parts of an ecosystem (sunlight, water, air, soil, rocks, temperature).
when living things depend on each other to survive.

sections.title: What is an ecosystem?
sections.lede: An ecosystem includes all the living things in an area and all the nonliving parts they interact with—like water, air, sunlight, and soil.

cards.title: Quick Facts
items: Ecosystems can be large (rainforests) or small (a pond).
Living things need nonliving resources to survive.
Ecosystems include plants, animals, and many other organisms.

cards.title: Look Closely
items: Name 3 living things that could live here.
Name 3 nonliving things you notice or can infer.
What makes this place a good home?

prompt.label: Prompt A — Define + Evidence
prompt.placeholder: Explain why this scene is an ecosystem. Name one living thing and one nonliving thing, and explain how they connect.
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Ecosystem = living + nonliving working together (example: plants use sunlight/water/soil).

sections.title: Biotic vs. Abiotic factors
sections.lede: Biotic factors are living things. Abiotic factors are nonliving parts like soil, water, sunlight, and temperature. Both affect survival.

cards.title: Biotic (Living)
items: Plants
Animals
Fungi
Bacteria (tiny living things)

cards.title: Abiotic (Nonliving)
items: Soil and rocks
Water
Air
Sunlight and temperature

sections.callout: Use the picture to support your choices—don’t guess without evidence.

prompt.label: Prompt B — Sort & Explain
prompt.placeholder:
One BIOTIC factor in this picture is: ____________
One ABIOTIC factor in this picture is: ____________
Explain how the abiotic factor helps the biotic factor survive: ____________________________
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Abiotic factors support life (soil holds nutrients/water; sunlight powers plant food-making).

sections.title: Interdependence: living things need each other
sections.lede: Many living things depend on each other for food, shelter, protection, or reproduction. Pollination is one example.

cards.title: Quick Facts
items: Bees collect nectar for food.
As bees move, they transfer pollen between flowers.
Pollination helps plants make seeds and fruit.

cards.title: Look Closely
items: What is the bee getting from the flower?
How is the flower helped by the bee?
Why is this relationship important?

prompt.label: Prompt C —Interdependence
prompt.placeholder: Describe how the bee and the flower depend on each other. What does each one get from this relationship?
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Bee gets food (nectar/pollen). Flower gets pollination so it can make seeds/fruit.

sections.title: What happens when ecosystems change?
sections.lede: When an abiotic factor changes—like water, temperature, or storms—living things may struggle to survive. Some move, adapt, or die.

cards.title: Examples
items: Less rain → fewer plants grow
Fewer plants → fewer animals can find food
Extreme heat or cold → some animals migrate

cards.title: Think Like a Scientist
items: What nonliving factor might have changed here?
Which living things would be affected first?
What could happen next in the ecosystem?

prompt.label: Prompt D — Cause & Effect
prompt.placeholder: Imagine this area gets much less water for a long time. Explain what could happen to plants first, then animals. Use step-by-step reasoning.
prompt.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Start with abiotic change → producers impacted → consumers impacted → ecosystem balance shifts.

sections.title: Quick Check + Create
sections.lede: Use the words ecosystem, biotic, abiotic, and interdependence.

cards.title: Mini Quiz
items: What is an ecosystem?
Name 2 abiotic factors.
What does interdependence mean?

cards.title: Challenge
items: Choose an ecosystem type (reef, desert, forest, pond).
List 2 biotic parts and 2 abiotic parts.
Describe one way living things depend on each other.

promptE.label: Prompt E — Summary
promptE.placeholder: Write a summary explaining what an ecosystem is, how biotic and abiotic factors work together, and why interdependence matters.
promptE.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Include living + nonliving, name examples, and explain dependence relationships.

promptF.label: Prompt F — Build Your Own Ecosystem
promptF.placeholder:
Ecosystem type: ____________
Biotic #1 (living): ____________
Biotic #2 (living): ____________
Abiotic #1 (nonliving): ____________
Abiotic #2 (nonliving): ____________
One interdependence link (who needs who and why): ____________________________
promptF.teacherKey: ✔ Key idea: Must include at least 2 living + 2 nonliving, plus a clear dependency link with a reason.
